title: Motion in One Dimension
content:
Motion in One Dimension

In this lecture, we will explore motion along a straight line, also known as one-dimensional motion. We'll apply the kinematic equations we learned previously to solve various problems.

Position-Time Graphs

A position-time graph shows how the position of an object changes over time. The slope of this graph at any point gives the instantaneous velocity at that time.

  • A horizontal line indicates zero velocity (the object is stationary).
  • A straight line with positive slope indicates constant positive velocity.
  • A straight line with negative slope indicates constant negative velocity.
  • A curved line indicates changing velocity (acceleration).

Velocity-Time Graphs

A velocity-time graph shows how the velocity of an object changes over time. The slope of this graph at any point gives the instantaneous acceleration at that time.

The area under a velocity-time graph between two time points gives the displacement during that time interval.

  • A horizontal line indicates zero acceleration (constant velocity).
  • A straight line with positive slope indicates constant positive acceleration.
  • A straight line with negative slope indicates constant negative acceleration.

Interactive Simulation

Below is an interactive simulation that demonstrates motion in one dimension. You can adjust the initial velocity and acceleration to see how they affect the motion of the object.

The simulation will load when you click the button below:

Example Problem

A car accelerates from rest at a constant rate of $3.0 \, \text{m/s}^2$. How far will it travel in 5.0 seconds?

We can use the equation:

$$x = x_0 + v_0t + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

With $x_0 = 0$, $v_0 = 0$, $a = 3.0 \, \text{m/s}^2$, and $t = 5.0 \, \text{s}$:

$$x = 0 + 0 \cdot 5.0 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot 3.0 \cdot (5.0)^2$$

$$x = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 3.0 \cdot 25.0 = 37.5 \, \text{m}$$

Therefore, the car will travel 37.5 meters in 5.0 seconds.
